basics.name: Lily Zhang
basics.interests: Riemannian foliations; Lie groupoids/algebroids; noncommutative geometry ($C^\ast$-algebras; $K$- and $KK$-theory); Poisson and symplectic geometry.

courses: Thesis Topic: On Molino’s Structure Theorem: From Riemannian Foliations to Riemannian Groupoids

projects.name: Master's Thesis
highlights: Reading literature on Lie groupoids and Riemannian Lie groupoids with the aim of understanding Molino’s Structure Theorem for Riemannian foliations and exploring potential extensions.
Developing preliminary notes translating the frame-bundle lift and basic foliation picture into Lie groupoid terms; assessing scope for possible generalizations.
description: Reading literature on Lie groupoids and Riemannian Lie groupoids with the aim of understanding Molino’s Structure Theorem for Riemannian foliations and exploring potential extensions.
Developing preliminary notes translating the frame-bundle lift and basic foliation picture into Lie groupoid terms; assessing scope for possible generalizations.

projects.name: Directed Reading — Supporting Master's Thesis
highlights: Completed a directed reading on orientations, manifold integration, de Rham cohomology, distributions/foliations, and quotient manifolds using J. M. Lee’s \textit{Introduction to Smooth Manifolds}—material beyond the Topology II (Differential Topology) syllabus that supplies the analytic toolkit for foliation theory.

Reading Moerdijk \& Mr\v{c}un’s \textit{Introduction to Foliations and Lie Groupoids} to understand (i) Molino’s theory for Riemannian foliations and (ii) the Lie groupoid description of holonomy and transverse geometry.
description: Completed a directed reading on orientations, manifold integration, de Rham cohomology, distributions/foliations, and quotient manifolds using J. M. Lee’s \textit{Introduction to Smooth Manifolds}—material beyond the Topology II (Differential Topology) syllabus that supplies the analytic toolkit for foliation theory.

Reading Moerdijk \& Mr\v{c}un’s \textit{Introduction to Foliations and Lie Groupoids} to understand (i) Molino’s theory for Riemannian foliations and (ii) the Lie groupoid description of holonomy and transverse geometry.

projects.name: Independent Study — K-Theory
highlights: Studying M. F. Atiyah's \textit{K-Theory}: vector bundles and the construction of $K^0$; reduced $K$ and long exact sequences; Bott periodicity; Thom isomorphism; computations on spheres, $\mathbb{CP}^n$, and Grassmannians; $\lambda$-operations and Adams operations; introduction to equivariant $K$-theory.
Exploring $K$-theory in noncommutative geometry (tentative).
description: Studying M. F. Atiyah's \textit{K-Theory}: vector bundles and the construction of $K^0$; reduced $K$ and long exact sequences; Bott periodicity; Thom isomorphism; computations on spheres, $\mathbb{CP}^n$, and Grassmannians; $\lambda$-operations and Adams operations; introduction to equivariant $K$-theory.
Exploring $K$-theory in noncommutative geometry (tentative).

projects.name: Directed Reading Program — Kasparov's KK-Theory
highlights: Studying Kasparov's $KK$-theory using B. Blackadar's \textit{K-Theory for Operator Algebras}: basic theory, intersection product, further structure in $KK$-theory, and equivariant $KK$-theory.
description: Studying Kasparov's $KK$-theory using B. Blackadar's \textit{K-Theory for Operator Algebras}: basic theory, intersection product, further structure in $KK$-theory, and equivariant $KK$-theory.

projects.name: Exploratory Project: Noncommutative Algebraic Geometry
projects.summary: Course: MATH 5333 (Graduate Algebraic Geometry). Advisor: Prof. Matt Kerr
highlights: Planned survey (in progress) of noncommutative algebraic geometry; tentatively exploring connections to Connes-style NCG and classical AG.
Annotated reading list in preparation (scheme-theoretic and operator-algebraic); possible short case studies time permitting.
description: Planned survey (in progress) of noncommutative algebraic geometry; tentatively exploring connections to Connes-style NCG and classical AG.
Annotated reading list in preparation (scheme-theoretic and operator-algebraic); possible short case studies time permitting.

projects.name: Guarding Orthogonal Art Galleries with Sliding Cameras
projects.summary: Course: MATH 437 (Computational Geometry). Advisor: Prof. Lori Ziegelmeier
highlights: MLSC on orthogonal polygons: reduced guarding to a minimum-weight vertex cover on a bipartite graph via the \(r\)-cut partition; for \emph{monotone} polygons the instance is \emph{convex} bipartite, giving an \(O(n\log^2 n)\) cover routine.
For FAT/MIN-AREA grid \(n\)-gons on a uniform grid, established equal-length segments, yielding an \emph{unweighted} convex bipartite instance with an \(O(n)\) cover step.
description: MLSC on orthogonal polygons: reduced guarding to a minimum-weight vertex cover on a bipartite graph via the \(r\)-cut partition; for \emph{monotone} polygons the instance is \emph{convex} bipartite, giving an \(O(n\log^2 n)\) cover routine.
For FAT/MIN-AREA grid \(n\)-gons on a uniform grid, established equal-length segments, yielding an \emph{unweighted} convex bipartite instance with an \(O(n)\) cover step.

projects.name: Cerebral Cortex: Geometric & Topological Models
projects.summary: Course: MATH 471 (Topology); Advisor: Prof. Lori Ziegelmeier
highlights: Graph-based topology correction for 3D cortical volumes: slice-wise foreground/background connectivity, max-spanning-tree pruning of weak links, and minimal voxel edits iterated across axes.
Validated via Marching Cubes meshes and Euler characteristic checks ($\chi=2$) on toy volumes and white-matter MRI masks.
description: Graph-based topology correction for 3D cortical volumes: slice-wise foreground/background connectivity, max-spanning-tree pruning of weak links, and minimal voxel edits iterated across axes.
Validated via Marching Cubes meshes and Euler characteristic checks ($\chi=2$) on toy volumes and white-matter MRI masks.

projects.name: Topics in Factorial Schur Functions
projects.summary: Course: MATH 5390 (Topics in Algebra). Advisor: Prof. John Shareshian
highlights: Survey of factorial Schur functions: definitions (ratio-of-alternants, tableaux), identities (Cauchy/dual Cauchy, Pieri-Giambelli), worked examples; annotated notes and computations (course project).

Variations and links: six-vertex/Yang-Baxter and Tokuyama-type formulas; K-theoretic analogues—factorial $P/Q$ for Schubert classes in $T$-equivariant $K$-theory of isotropic Grassmannians, with Pfaffian and set-valued tableau formulas and the GKM/divided-difference framework.
description: Survey of factorial Schur functions: definitions (ratio-of-alternants, tableaux), identities (Cauchy/dual Cauchy, Pieri-Giambelli), worked examples; annotated notes and computations (course project).

Variations and links: six-vertex/Yang-Baxter and Tokuyama-type formulas; K-theoretic analogues—factorial $P/Q$ for Schubert classes in $T$-equivariant $K$-theory of isotropic Grassmannians, with Pfaffian and set-valued tableau formulas and the GKM/divided-difference framework.

projects.name: Understanding Burnside's Theorem
projects.summary: Course: MATH 476 (Representation Theory). Advisor: Prof. Tom Halverson
highlights: Expository, character-theoretic proof of Burnside’s $p^a q^b$ theorem via Sylow theory, the class equation, column orthogonality, and algebraic-integer methods.
Proved supporting lemmas (e.g., $|\chi(g)|\le \chi(1)$ and a conjugacy-class size criterion) and deduced solvability of groups of order $p^a q^b$.
description: Expository, character-theoretic proof of Burnside’s $p^a q^b$ theorem via Sylow theory, the class equation, column orthogonality, and algebraic-integer methods.
Proved supporting lemmas (e.g., $|\chi(g)|\le \chi(1)$ and a conjugacy-class size criterion) and deduced solvability of groups of order $p^a q^b$.

projects.name: Baire Category Theorem and Its Applications
projects.summary: Course: MATH 477 (Projects in Analysis). Advisor: Prof. Taryn Flock
highlights: Exposition of the Baire Category Theorem (equivalent formulations) with applications to the Uniform Boundedness Principle and the Closed Graph Theorem.
Worked examples: a generic but measure-zero set; Grothendieck’s theorem on closed subspaces of $L^p$ contained in $L^\infty$.
description: Exposition of the Baire Category Theorem (equivalent formulations) with applications to the Uniform Boundedness Principle and the Closed Graph Theorem.
Worked examples: a generic but measure-zero set; Grothendieck’s theorem on closed subspaces of $L^p$ contained in $L^\infty$.

projects.name: An Algorithmic Approach to Knapsack Problems
highlights: Surveyed $0$–$1$, fractional, and MDKP; implemented DP, a scaling FPTAS, and a $1/2$-approx greedy; proved fractional greedy optimal.
Built Python benchmarks for MDKP and baselines; explored GA/local-search heuristics.
description: Surveyed $0$–$1$, fractional, and MDKP; implemented DP, a scaling FPTAS, and a $1/2$-approx greedy; proved fractional greedy optimal.
Built Python benchmarks for MDKP and baselines; explored GA/local-search heuristics.

projects.name: Understanding Housing Bubble in Vancouver, BC: A Time Series Analysis
projects.summary: Course: STAT 452 (Correlated Data); Advisor: Prof. Brianna Heggeseth
highlights: Log-HPI (2005--2021): ADF; ACF/PACF-guided SARIMA $(2,1,0)\times(2,0,0)_{12}$ chosen by AIC/BIC; Ljung--Box diagnostics; 12-month forecasts.

VAR(1) on $\Delta$HPI, GDP, 10-year yield, CAD/USD, employment, earnings, CPI, crime + Bill~28 dummy; Granger tests, Cholesky IRFs, FEVD; results: HPI variance mostly self-driven; GDP predictive (95\%); employment marginal (90\%); CPI/yield shocks short-run; policy coefficient negative/significant.
description: Log-HPI (2005--2021): ADF; ACF/PACF-guided SARIMA $(2,1,0)\times(2,0,0)_{12}$ chosen by AIC/BIC; Ljung--Box diagnostics; 12-month forecasts.

VAR(1) on $\Delta$HPI, GDP, 10-year yield, CAD/USD, employment, earnings, CPI, crime + Bill~28 dummy; Granger tests, Cholesky IRFs, FEVD; results: HPI variance mostly self-driven; GDP predictive (95\%); employment marginal (90\%); CPI/yield shocks short-run; policy coefficient negative/significant.

projects.name: Cozy Home
projects.summary: Course: MATH 432 (Mathematical Modeling); Advisor: Prof. Matt Zumbrum
highlights: ODE indoor-climate model with Fourier heat loss and window air exchange; device effects via sparse $M_1,M_2$ on binary $Y$; quadratic discomfort with PD $S_i$; derived $c_{\mathrm{opt}}=(\sum_i S_i)^{-1}\sum_i S_i c_{p,i}$ and $S_{\mathrm{eff}}=\sum_i S_i$.

Time-weighted least squares (daily/weekly/annual kernels) to estimate $c_{\mathrm{opt}}(t)$, $S_{\mathrm{eff}}(t)$, and conductivity $\sigma$; implemented a thresholded control rule for device switching.
description: ODE indoor-climate model with Fourier heat loss and window air exchange; device effects via sparse $M_1,M_2$ on binary $Y$; quadratic discomfort with PD $S_i$; derived $c_{\mathrm{opt}}=(\sum_i S_i)^{-1}\sum_i S_i c_{p,i}$ and $S_{\mathrm{eff}}=\sum_i S_i$.

Time-weighted least squares (daily/weekly/annual kernels) to estimate $c_{\mathrm{opt}}(t)$, $S_{\mathrm{eff}}(t)$, and conductivity $\sigma$; implemented a thresholded control rule for device switching.

projects.name: Contributed Talk — Two Classical Theorems in Codimension-1 Foliations
highlights: Introduce and sketch proofs of Haefliger’s and Novikov’s theorems, with illustrative examples.
Emphasis on holonomy, vanishing cycles, Reeb components, and the role of (foliated) Morse functions.
description: Introduce and sketch proofs of Haefliger’s and Novikov’s theorems, with illustrative examples.
Emphasis on holonomy, vanishing cycles, Reeb components, and the role of (foliated) Morse functions.
